content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers XXX and YYY. You need to construct two different strings S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​ consisting of only the characters ‘a’‘a’‘a’ and ‘b’‘b’‘b’, such that the following conditions are satisfied:


Both S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​ are palindromes.
Both S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​ should contain exactly XXX occurrences of a\texttt{a}a and YYY occurrences of b\texttt{b}b.
If there are multiple possible answers, you may print **any** of them. If it is not possible to construct two distinct strings satisfying the conditions, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing two space-separated integers X,YX, YX,Y.


Output Format
For each test case:



If it is not possible to construct two distinct strings satisfying the conditions, print −1-1−1
Otherwise, print two lines — the first should contain the string S1S_1S1​ and the second should contain S2S_2S2​
If there are multiple possible answers, you may print any of them.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, if it is impossible to output two distinct palindromes with exactly X letters 'a' and Y letters 'b', print a single line containing -1. Otherwise print two lines: the first line is any valid palindrome S1, the second line is another valid palindrome S2 (S1 ≠ S2).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: X=2, Y=4 are both even, so a palindrome exists. aHalf=1, bHalf=2. First half "abb" gives palindrome "abbbba". Swapping halves gives "bbaabb". Both satisfy the counts and are different. Test case 2: X=3, Y=3 are both odd, therefore no palindrome can be formed, so output is -1.

solution.approach: A palindrome can be formed iff at most one of the character counts is odd. Therefore if both X and Y are odd the answer is -1. Even when a palindrome exists we need at least two different ones. This is impossible when one character does not appear at all (X=0 or Y=0) or when the only odd count equals 1 (X=1 or Y=1) because the centre is forced and the remaining half is forced. In all other feasible cases we can build two distinct palindromes by arranging the first half differently. Let aHalf = X/2, bHalf = Y/2 (integer division). If X is odd the centre character is 'a', else if Y is odd the centre is 'b', otherwise there is no centre. First palindrome: first half = aHalf times 'a' followed by bHalf times 'b'. Second palindrome: first half = bHalf times 'b' followed by aHalf times 'a'. Append the centre (if any) and the reverse of the first half to obtain the full strings. Because both aHalf and bHalf are positive in the feasible region, the two palindromes are guaranteed to be different.
solution.timeComplexity: O(X+Y) per test case – we only construct strings of length X+Y.
